title: Second Moment of the GL_3 Standard L-function on the Critical Line
speaker: Matthew Young
abstract:
The second and fourth moments of the Riemann zeta function have been known for about a century, but the sixth moment remains elusive.  
The sixth moment of zeta can be thought of as the second moment of a GL_3 Eisenstein series, and it is natural to consider variants of the problem
where the Eisenstein series is replaced by a cusp form.  I will discuss recent work with Agniva Dasgupta and Wing Hong Leung where we obtain a
nontrivial bound on this second moment.  I will also discuss some applications, including an improvement on the Rankin-Selberg problem.

proof_stages.label: Moment and automorphic-L-function motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames bounds for L-functions through moments, starting from zeta on the critical line and then using GL2 and GL3 automorphic analogies to identify the fixed GL3 standard L-function as the target.

proof_stages.label: Main GL3 second-moment theorem and calibration
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: for a fixed GL3 cusp form on SL3(Z), the second moment on the critical line is bounded by T^{4/3}, which is subconvex relative to the T^3 analytic conductor and improves on the trivial T^{3/2} mean-value bound.

proof_stages.label: Consequences via spectral reciprocity and Rankin-Selberg factorization
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is placed in the history of GL3 subconvexity and then applied, through spectral reciprocity and through the factorization zeta times symmetric square, to obtain improved average/subconvex consequences and a Rankin-Selberg error-term improvement.

proof_stages.label: Approximate functional equation and shifted-convolution reduction
proof_stages.summary: Using the approximate functional equation, squaring, and integrating the oscillatory factors, the second moment is reduced to an averaged shifted convolution sum of GL3 Fourier coefficients with shifts |m-n| of size about N/T.

proof_stages.label: DFI delta symbol with an unbalanced modulus choice
proof_stages.summary: The DFI delta method is inserted to detect the shifted equation m=n+k, but with Q chosen smaller than the standard square-root length, trading larger Archimedean oscillation for smaller arithmetic moduli.

proof_stages.label: Voronoi-Poisson transformation to a dual bilinear form
proof_stages.summary: GL3 Voronoi summation in the GL3-coefficient variable and Poisson summation in the shift variable transform the problem into a dual bilinear form involving Kloosterman-type sums, after which the GL3 coefficients are discarded in favor of a general bilinear-form estimate.

proof_stages.label: Reciprocity, character separation, and large sieve endgame
proof_stages.summary: After duality and another Poisson step, elementary reciprocity changes the moduli, multiplicative-character expansion separates the variables, and a large-sieve estimate together with usable Weil-type bounds for twisted Kloosterman sums yields the optimized Q and the T^{4/3} bound.

proof_stages.label: Methodological payoff and variants
proof_stages.summary: The proof is contrasted with the earlier recursive Cauchy-Schwarz/Deligne-bound approach, and the speaker explains that the large-sieve replacement should also recover short-interval results and suggests variants such as Dirichlet twists with factorable modulus.

examples.name: Riemann zeta moments as the low-rank model
examples.note: The zeta-function moment problem, including the Hardy Z-function plot and the known second/fourth/twelfth moment bounds, supplies a familiar comparison for why second moments of higher-degree L-functions matter, but it is not used in the GL3 proof.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses zeta moments as the model, deriving tail bounds from the second/fourth moments, comparing convexity and short-moment exponents, and interpolating known moments to get a sixth-moment exponent.

examples.name: Function-field zeta functions of curves
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker compares number-field moment problems with zeta functions of smooth projective curves over finite fields, emphasizing that RH-type input does not make exact moment asymptotics easy.
examples.engagement_reason: Function-field zeta functions are only discussed in response to a question via general remarks about RH, upper bounds, and moment conjectures, with no concrete curve or calculation worked out.

passes.reason: Function-field zeta functions of curves are a standard infinite-family analogue invoked for comparison, not a sporadic or adversarial case.

examples.name: GL2 cusp-form L-functions and the Eisenstein/zeta-squared analogy
examples.note: The GL2 cusp-form second moment is presented as the cuspidal analogue of the fourth moment of zeta, helping motivate the passage from zeta powers to automorphic L-functions.
examples.engagement_reason: He writes the GL2 cusp-form Fourier expansion, rescales coefficients to form the degree-two L-function, and concretely checks the Eisenstein/divisor-coefficient analogy leading to zeta squared and the zeta-fourth-moment analogue.

examples.name: Divisor-problem comparison for Rankin-Selberg errors
examples.note: An audience question compares the Rankin-Selberg error-term corollary with divisor-problem/Eisenstein-series methods, serving only as a contextual check on the corollary.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies the second-moment theorem to the Rankin-Selberg error problem by converting the coefficient sum to an integral, factoring it as zeta times symmetric-square, and extracting the improved four-sevenths exponent while comparing it to divisor-problem methods.

passes.reason: The divisor-problem comparison is a standard analogue for Rankin-Selberg error estimates and is not used as a counterexample.

examples.name: Oscillatory versus periodic conductor behavior
examples.note: The comparison between functions like n^{iy}, whose product still oscillates at about frequency y, and periodic functions modulo q1 and q2, whose periods multiply, explains why shifting conductor from arithmetic moduli into Archimedean oscillation can be advantageous.
examples.engagement_reason: He explicitly reasons through the example of multiplying oscillatory factors versus periodic functions, comparing Archimedean conductor staying at the maximum with arithmetic periods combining by lcm/product.

passes.reason: The oscillatory-versus-periodic conductor comparison is a typical heuristic example explaining conductor tradeoffs rather than an object with special classification status.
The oscillatory and periodic products are ordinary illustrative cases of conductor behavior rather than structurally special objects.
The oscillatory-function example is used to show that the finite-place heuristic that conductors multiply can fail at the Archimedean place.
